strengths: The narrative progression is logical and well-structured, clearly moving from the problem of non-Lambertian reflectance to the limitations of existing methods, and finally to the proposed dual-mask physical model.
Data consistency between the abstract and the introduction is well-maintained, with specific MAE improvements (47.9% and 7.4%) matching the baseline metrics provided in the text.
The motivation for combining MRI-inspired angular frequency analysis with component-aware fusion is technically sound and addresses a significant gap in current light field depth estimation research.
weaknesses: The citation keys are clearly placeholders generated by an automated process (e.g., \cite{lightlight}, \cite{nonnon}, \cite{epiepi}), indicating that the bibliography is either missing, incomplete, or fabricated, which critically undermines the scholarly rigor.

There is a complete absence of mathematical formalization in the provided text; the methodology relies entirely on descriptive text without utilizing proper \begin{equation} environments to define the dual-mask model or the loss functions.
The source code lacks the experimental section, figures, and tables. Without actual visual evidence or detailed ablation studies, the claimed performance improvements cannot be verified.
key_gaps_vs_tcsvt: Missing mathematical formulations and proper equation environments, which are mandatory for a theoretical physical modeling paper in TCSVT.
Placeholder/dummy citation keys (e.g., \cite{epiepi}) suggest an incomplete manuscript, failing the journal's basic peer-review integrity and reference formatting standards.

Absence of any experimental figures, TikZ diagrams of the network architecture, or result tables in the provided LaTeX source, preventing any assessment of visual or quantitative comparative performance.
